name: British English (RP)
notes: Received Pronunciation (RP) — traditional prestige accent of England. NON-RHOTIC: /r/ deleted before consonants and word-finally. TRAP-BATH split: BATH words = /ɑː/ (castle, grass, dance). LOT = /ɒ/ (rounded); GOAT = /əʊ/. T-glottaling common in contemporary RP (glottal stop for /t/ word-finally). Source: Wells (1982) vol. 1–2, Cruttenden (2014). C/G SOFTENING: c→[s] and g→[dʒ] before e/i/y (city, gem); exceptions: get, give, girl retain [ɡ]. INTERVOCALIC s: [z] between vowels (rose, nose) but [s] word-initially. TH DISTINCTION: [θ] in content words, [ð] in function words and intervocalically. X WORD-INITIAL: [z] (xylophone) vs [ks] elsewhere. SILENT FINAL E: orthographic word-final <e> after a consonant is not pronounced (mate, hope, time, judge); it historically marked the preceding vowel as tense/long, but that vowel-quality shift is not encoded here because the engine's positional model has no lookback to the preceding nucleus (known engine-limit exception). A small closed class of function words spelled with a genuinely pronounced final <e> (the, be, he, me, we, she) is carved out via `word_exceptions`, since the blanket positional rule cannot distinguish these monosyllables from the regular silent-e pattern. TION/SION FAMILY: -tion/-cian -> [ʃən], -ssion -> [ʃən] (mission, passion; matched via the dedicated `ssion` grapheme so maximal-munch tokenization picks it over `sion`), -sion -> [ʒən] after a vowel (vision, division, decision) or [ʃən] after a consonant (tension, pension, mansion), modelled with the `sion` entry in `positional_graphemes` using AFTER_VOWEL/AFTER_CONSONANT context, -tial/-cial -> [ʃəl], -cious/-tious -> [ʃəs] (Cruttenden 2014 spelling-to-sound correspondence rules). LEXICAL STRESS: word stress is lexically contrastive (record noun vs. record verb) and not reliably orthography-predictable the way Romance penult/final stress endings are, so no declarative `stress` block is encoded (research-tier stress exemption per docs/quality_tiers.md; stress is left to downstream lexical lookup rather than a spelling rule). CANDIDATE WEIGHTS: a few ambiguous graphemes carry per-candidate `weights` (candidate frequencies) so the beam favours the corpus-dominant phoneme rather than the declared-first one — `er` = [ɜːɹ 0.2, əɹ 0.8] (unstressed schwar dominates the lexicon), `gh` = [ɡ 0.03, f 0.12, silent 0.85] (⟨gh⟩ is silent in the vast majority of words: night, though, high, weigh), `ie` = [aɪ 0.4, iː 0.6] (the /iː~i/ realisation of -ie/-ies edges out /aɪ/). Values are approximate frequencies grounded in Gontijo et al. (2003) British-English and Berndt et al. (1987) grapheme-phoneme probability studies, not PER-tuned parameters; graphemes without weights keep uniform rank ordering. See docs/candidate_scoring.md. Suffix spellings (⟨-tion⟩ ⟨-cious⟩ ⟨-tial⟩ …) are morphology, not graphemes: they are not units of the writing system and cannot be stated over phonological classes. English orthography is deep, so a lexicon or a morphological analyser in a downstream consumer of the lattice is the right place for them, not this spec.

sources.author: Wells, J.C.
sources.title: Accents of English, Vol. 1: An Introduction

sources.author: Wells, J.C.
sources.title: Accents of English, Vol. 2: The British Isles

sources.author: Roach, P.
sources.title: British English: Received Pronunciation
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association, 34(2), 239-245
sources.notes: Illustrations of the IPA series entry; RP phoneme inventory and worked transcription.

sources.author: Gontijo, P.F.D., Gontijo, I., & Shillcock, R.
sources.title: Grapheme-phoneme probabilities in British English
sources.notes: Corpus-derived conditional probabilities of grapheme-to-phoneme correspondences in British English. Basis for the `weights` on the `er`, `gh` and `ie` grapheme entries: candidate frequencies, not tuned parameters.

sources.author: Berndt, R.S., Reggia, J.A., & Mitchum, C.C.
sources.title: Empirically derived probabilities for grapheme-to-phoneme correspondences in English
sources.notes: Grapheme-to-phoneme correspondence probabilities from a 17,310-word corpus. Corroborates the dominant correspondences encoded in the weighted grapheme entries (e.g. `gh` most frequently silent; unstressed `er` = schwar).
